title: A Simple GPU-Accelerated Solver for the Schrödinger Operator with Applications to Ground States and Hamiltonian Simulation
abstract: We extend the tensor-product direct solver from the Laplacian to the Schrödinger operator $-Δ+ V$. When the potential $V_1$ is separable, the operator $-Δ+ V_1$ is inverted or exponentiated at cost $O(N^{1+1/d})$ in $d$ dimensions via per-axis eigendecomposition. On a single NVIDIA A100 GPU, this costs less than one second for $10^9$ degrees of freedom in 3D. For non-separable potentials $V = V_1 + V_2$, the same solver provides a preconditioner $(-Δ+ V_1)^{-1}$ for the preconditioned conjugate gradient (PCG) method and a propagator for operator-splitting time integrators. For bounded $V_2$, we prove that the preconditioned operator has a bounded condition number and a clustered spectrum with at most finitely many outlier eigenvalues, independently of the mesh size, and also independently of the domain size when $V_1$ is a confining potential. This explains the mesh- and domain-independent PCG iteration counts observed in practice. We apply this method to ground state computation via inverse iteration for linear problems and via the $a_u$ gradient flow for Gross--Pitaevskii energy in 3D, and also Hamiltonian simulation via the approximated qHOP and Magnus-2 splitting methods from 3D to 9D on a single NVIDIA GH200 GPU.
summary: The paper introduces a highly efficient GPU-accelerated solver for the Schrödinger operator that scales exceptionally well to billions of degrees of freedom. By using a tensor-product preconditioner, the authors enable rapid ground state computation and Hamiltonian simulation for complex, non-separable, and high-dimensional quantum systems.
main_problem: Developing an efficient, GPU-accelerated solver for the Schrödinger operator, specifically addressing the loss of tensor-product structure when dealing with non-separable potentials.
main_result: The authors present a solver that achieves $O(N^{1+1/d})$ complexity and can invert operators with $10^9$ degrees of freedom in under one second on an NVIDIA A100. They also prove that the proposed preconditioner ensures grid-independent convergence and a bounded condition number.
method: A tensor-product direct solver using per-axis eigendecomposition for separable potentials, combined with a Preconditioned Conjugate Gradient (PCG) method using the separable part as a preconditioner for non-separable potentials. The implementation uses the Spectral Element Method (SEM) and JAX on NVIDIA GPUs.
model_or_system: The Schrödinger operator $H = -\Delta + V$, including separable and non-separable potentials, the Gross-Pitaevskii equation (GPE), and multi-body Coulomb Hamiltonians in 4D, 6D, and 9D.
key_observables: Ground state energy, eigenvalues, wavefunction profiles, and inter-particle correlations.
important_parameters: Dimension $d$, nonlinearity strength $ eta$, mesh size $h$, domain size $L$, and the Coulomb singularity regularization parameter $\delta$.
main_assumptions: The potential $V$ can be split into a separable part $V_1$ and a bounded non-separable part $V_2$; domains are primarily rectangular with homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions.
figures_summary: Figures show PCG convergence for various potentials, the evolution of ground state profiles as nonlinearity increases, and visual slices of particle densities in 6D and 9D systems.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the extension of the tensor-product solver to the Schrödinger operator, provides mathematical proofs for spectral clustering and preconditioner effectiveness, demonstrates high-performance GPU implementation, and applies the method to ground state computation (GPE) and high-dimensional Hamiltonian simulation.
why_interesting: This work provides a highly scalable computational framework for simulating many-body quantum dynamics and Bose-Einstein condensates, enabling the study of high-dimensional systems (up to 9D) that were previously computationally inaccessible.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1 also shows that the performance of the preconditioner (−∆+ V1)−1 is independent of both the domain size L and the mesh size h for the stirrer potential, while for the quartic potential, the iteration count grows with L.

Fig. 1. PCG convergence (relative residual vs. iteration) for (−∆+ V1 + V2) u = f, Q6 SEM, FP32, GH200. Top row: quartic potential (3.1) (left) and stirrer potential (3.2) (right), both at n = 7013, comparing the proposed preconditioner (−∆+ V1)−1 with the (−∆)−1 or PC. Bottom row: domain dependence of (−∆+ V1)−1 for the quartic potential (left, L = 8, 10, 30, 50) and the stirrer potential (right, L = 8, 10, 100). Total GPU time for each case is shown in the legend.

Fig. 2. Cross-sections of u at x=0, y=0, z=0, plotted on [−6, 6]3. Q20 SEM, 1993 DoFs, computed on [−8, 8]3. Each panel uses its own color scale. Left: eigenfunction initial guess (linear ground state, β=0). Center: GPE ground state, β=100. Right: GPE ground state, β=1600.
Fig. 3. Comparison of au flow and modified H1 flow for β = 100, Q20 SEM, 5993 ≈2.1 × 108
Fig. 4. Comparison of au flow and modified H1 flow for β = 1600, Q20 SEM, 9993 ≈109
